data.title: BRBI BR Partners: New IPO On Nasdaq Offers Growth And Income In Brazil
data.description:
The photo below is from BRBI BR Partners' (NASDAQ:BRBI) 2021 earnings presentation. In addition to being a little nostalgic for me, it proves that I have known the company for some time, since around its IPO in Brazil in 2021. And now, it is time for the IPO on Nasdaq, with the aim of attracting foreign investors who recognize the bank's potential.

Although there are uncertainties surrounding the thesis, it combines the two main attractive factors in equity investment, growth and income, and here I am not talking about dividend growth, but rather a company that, in addition to having a double-digit dividend yield, continues to have very high potential.

My take is that, in addition to this long-term potential, the BR Partners thesis is also an “undiscovered play,” and still has a lot to capture in terms of coverage in the US and Brazil, and consequently, improve liquidity and re-rating of the valuation.

That said, I assign a buy rating to BRBI stock and consider it to be among my best GARP ideas.

A Little About BR Partners

I believe that many are unfamiliar with BR Partners. It is an investment bank from Brazil, and despite being both a “recent” IPO (in 2021) and a small bank ($350 million market cap), it is already operationally robust and has a complete advisory portfolio, as the table below shows.

In the image below, you can see the company's history, from its founding by Ricardo Lacerda to its IPO, which is where the company gained more firepower to expand its products more rapidly. It is interesting to note that not only has the portfolio (image above) evolved, but many efforts and advances have been made in areas such as liquidity/governance, as illustrated by the follow-on and Nasdaq listing.

Discussing wide moats for this type of niche is a little more difficult and debatable, but I believe that BR Partners has built some relevant ones. The first is its ownership culture, and the acquisition and, above all, retention of talent in this industry is essential. The company already has elaborate models for compensation, partnership, and alignment of long-term interests.

Linked to this is the bank's reputation, which has already proven itself as an independent player, having advised on several important deals in Brazilian industry and now building an important network of contacts, often creating something analogous to a switching cost, due to the length of its relationship with some customers.

In turn, there is synergistic potential between the areas. The ecosystem is already complete, and therefore, the company can benefit in various scenarios and act in a complementary manner. For example, after selling a company, the customer may be interested in wealth management, just as a wealth management customer may be interested in IPOs, PE, and other types of operations.

This complete portfolio also mitigates one of the main risks of the thesis, which is the complexity of the Brazilian macroeconomy. Although we are not seeing IPOs in the market, and high uncertainty and high interest rates are restricting M&A, the company is still able to sustain its revenue through debt issuance (Capital Market).

Financials and Key Info About BR Partners

In the last earnings call, the bank made it clear that this is one of the worst moments for investment banking, especially for M&A. Still, the financials are far from bad. Between 2020 and 2024, revenue showed a CAGR of 24%, and for 2025 it will likely be closer to flat. Revenue per managing director has recently remained at R$45 million, even in a more challenging period.

It is worth mentioning that the compensation ratio in 2024 was just under 25%, and the company's limit is 30%. Meanwhile, all of its peers had an indicator above 50%. This is very important, since revenue per MD is not that far off, surpassing companies such as Moelis & Co. (MC) and Houlihan Lokey (HLI).

Below, you can see how other key indicators have been performing recently. The YoY decline in revenue can be largely attributed to lower IB and CM revenue, as well as lower capital revenue in the period, while Treasury Sales & Structuring and Wealth Management, which are less representative, continue to grow at double digits (with WM standing out with 32% YoY growth).

Note that although it is a small company, ROAE is exceptional, remaining above 20% and at times approaching the ROAE of BTG Pactual, which is infinitely larger, with revenue more than 50 times greater. Another point to highlight is the Basel ratio. The indicator is above 20%, well above the Brazilian Central Bank's minimum requirement of 11%. This shows both conservatism in the balance sheet and room for growth.

This is especially important because part of BRBI' business is “balance sheet as a service.” BR Partners can place part of the debt it issues in its portfolio, for example, to assist with liquidity. In other words, sometimes, in addition to earning fees from the CM process, the company can also earn by retaining part of the security.

This means that BRBI shareholders are also indirectly exposed to Brazil's fixed income market. For example, BR Partners recently coordinated the issuance of a bond by Dotz S.A., which pays DI + 8% per annum and matures in 2029. “DI” is only a few bps lower than the Selic Rate, which is 15% per year, i.e., it is an extremely high-yield security. I will not go into whether or not it is a good security, since much of Dotz's history is a net loss, and only recently in Q2 did the company manage to present a more significant net income.

Triggers & Growth Opportunities for BR Partners

To understand the bank's main growth opportunities, it is necessary to understand the context of the Brazilian market. Its main activities, such as M&A, are directly affected by Brazil's high interest rates, as well as by the landscape of uncertainty.

The Selic Rate (Brazil's FFR) is at 15% per year, an extremely restrictive level and one of the highest in recent history, as you can see in the chart below.

One might think that this high risk-free rate comes with a high inflation outlook, but that is not quite the case. Despite the uncertainties and debates, the outlook is for inflation (IPCA) to end 2025 at 4.8%. This creates one of the highest real interest rates in the world, which consequently slows down the economy and completely changes risk appetite.

The consequence for the BR Partners market is obvious. Even with so many companies issuing debt at such a high cost of debt, and some M&As being done, this macroeconomic scenario is under pressure and far from its full potential. There have been no IPOs on the Brazilian stock exchange for years.

This creates pent-up demand for BR Partners, which is one of the well-positioned banks in this market, to capture when the cycle improves. The Selic rate is expected to continue falling in the coming years, although still at high levels, but it is well known that Brazil is a cyclical country. When the perceived fiscal risk decreases and interest rates fall to a more acceptable level, companies that have postponed their IPOs should “rush” to complete them within an acceptable window, and the same is true for debt issuance, M&As, and even risk appetite and mark-to-market gains for other revenues.

In addition to the turnaround in the macroeconomic scenario, there are several opportunities for growth in the Brazilian market and BR Partners' market share. The company already has a significant market share in some markets, such as 27% of the independent advisors market and M&A, but in my view, there is still plenty of room for players like BR Partners to gain ground as the market becomes less fragmented.

The most emblematic is Wealth Management, where the company has only 2.4%, so it has the potential to boost this market share in the long run. The market is no longer small, as BTG Pactual's revenue of more than R$4 billion from wealth management and personal banking alone shows, but it still has a lot of room to mature as factors such as economic cycles, financial education, and the like play out in the Brazilian economy.

In other words, I believe that the prospects for top-line growth and, consequently, for value creation for the bank are positive for the next years, not to mention optionalities such as specific niches that the bank may begin to explore, internationalization, and other trends such as possible waves of privatizations and other state assets, depending on the governments in the coming years.

The Main Risks for BR Partners

In my view, there are three main risks to BR Partners' thesis. The first is what I mentioned above as the potential held back by the Brazilian market. Although there are signs that interest rates will fall over time, there is no guarantee that the fiscal scenario will improve. It is not impossible that worse cycles, such as the one currently affecting the country, will continue for some time or even slow down a little more. In this scenario, the company may lose revenue and margin and even see its multiples compressed given the expectations and uncertainty surrounding its growth.

The second risk, which I believe is more important, is competition and concentration. Although Brazil is large and has several companies and sectors that make the IB and CM markets more heated, the big players matter a lot. In other words, if relationships with strategic clients are lost, this could substantially hurt revenue, and this is closely linked to intense competition in this sector, ranging from big players such as BTG Pactual to smaller boutiques such as BRBI.

The third latent risk to the business is its dependence on talent. The bank has grown well in recent years, but the number of MDs and partners is still small. Therefore, if any key senior staff leave, retire, or similar, this could have some level of impact on the relationship network.

Finally, it is worth mentioning some more generic risks, such as the exchange rate risk in Brazil (and you will see that in the section below, I will do the valuation in BRL), and the greater volatility due to it being not only a Brazilian stock, but a small cap with more limited liquidity.

The good news is that the company is actively working to resolve this second point, as illustrated by ADR's Nasdaq listing. The company aims to expand its shareholder base and improve liquidity and has spoken with a number of funds focused on small caps and emerging markets. It is worth mentioning that two American small-cap funds have begun to build positions. With this greater institutional and American visibility, perhaps the valuation gap compared to peers may narrow.

BRBI’s Valuation: GARP or Deep Value?

Based on revenue and margin trends, I believe that BR Partners’ net income should be close to R$170 million in 2025. This implies a P/E ratio below 11x, as well as the prospect of a high dividend yield.

Comparing this multiple with that of Moelis & Co, Lazard (LAZ), Evercore (EVR), PJT Partners (PJT), Jefferies Financial Group (JEF), and Houlihan Lokey, it is clear that there is a distortion in the valuation. Of these, the multiple that most closely approximates that of BR Partners is Lazard's forward P/E ratio of 22.5x, which is still twice as high.

With that, one could argue that BR Partners is a deep value case. It is greatly undervalued in relation to its peers, and just a re-rating caused by an improvement in the economic cycle and better institutional presence could unlock an upside of more than 50% without much optimism.

But in my view, on the spectrum between Deep Value and GARP, BR Partners is closer to GARP. We must remember that although the multiple is low, we are dealing with a Brazilian small cap. ROAE is high, but we must remember that Brazil's risk-free rate is 15%, so the discount rate is also quite high. Therefore, when compared to Brazilian players, the valuation gap is less apparent.

BTG Pactual trades at around 14x earnings, while Itaú Unibanco (ITUB), Brazil's most traditional retail bank, trades at around 10x.

Still, I believe that as BRBI builds better relationships with the US market, it is possible that its valuation will be better priced. Nu Holdings (NU) and MercadoLibre (MELI) are good examples of growth stories that, despite their significant exposure to Brazil, have managed to convince global investors to pay higher multiples to compensate for quality and growth.

In any case, I prefer to be conservative when trying to find the fair intrinsic value of BR Partners, and not count on a re-rating of its multiple to unlock all this upside in the short term.

For the assumptions, I considered revenue growth of 1% for next year, given the uncertainties, election year, and growth trend in recent quarters. After that, I considered a recovery in 2027 and 2028, and after that, a normalization to growth of only 5% per year, which is only slightly above Brazilian inflation and well below the full potential of the bank and the industry.

For the margin, I assumed a peak of 38% in 2028 (below what the company has already achieved in its best quarters), stabilizing at 35%.

Considering a terminal growth of 3% and discounting this net income flow by 15% (above Brazil's fixed-rate Treasury), we have an upside of 8%, even though we are being very conservative and using a high discount rate.

This proxy for a fair market cap of R$2.04 billion (~$385 million) implies that the IRR in BRL is 16%, an attractive level that shows how the company has the potential for compounding in the coming years.

Although it is difficult to have strong conviction in the Brazilian exchange rate, even if there is a slight devaluation of the BRL during this period, it already seems worth the risk. In fact, the Focus Report expects the exchange rate to remain stable until 2028, but given the current interest rates and uncertainties, I believe there will be at least a gradual devaluation in the long term due to the inflation differential.

The Bottom Line

In short, I believe that even with the uncertainties and challenges of the Brazilian scenario and exchange rate, BR Partners is a very compelling thesis in a growing market in Brazil.

Of course, the risks and volatility are higher than usual here, but a high IRR and considerable upside potential through re-rating and growth balance this out, making the risk-reward ratio attractive, especially when we consider that BRBI may be a potential GARP and already has a dividend yield close to double digits.

“Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!”

data.title: Ur-Energy Turns A Corner Operationally, But Not Yet Financially
data.description:
After quite long of stagnation and losses Ur-Energy (NYSE:URG) finally showed real progress. In Q2 2025, the company made $10.4 million in revenue – this is first time the company is not only talking but showing that it can make money, but not yet the profits. The revenue mostly came from production and active deliveries, that is why it's first time. Lost Creek project, which was also stagnating, is not now only on paper, but showing results.

In a quarter they packed 112 thousand pounds of U₃O₈ and sold 105 thousand – this is +35% more than in Q1. So, this trend is showing that the company is nearing its annual anticipated plan – 440 thousand pounds. This is important not only for revenue, but for liquidity as well: this will let the company sell the unsold inventory (~400 thousand pounds).

Another signal – the costs are moving in the right direction. In 2024 one-unit cost was almost $50/lb, now in 2025 it is slightly reduced to $42.83/lb. If we compare it to the average sales price under the long-term contracts $61.56/lb, this gives a gross margin of around $18.73lb. This doesn't account for corporate overhead or capital costs just yet, but it shows that the company is progressing toward positive gross margin.

At the same time Shirley Basin project is being built according to plans – they have already built operations building, brought in main equipment and some of the drillings are starting as well. The main start is expected in early 2026 and from what I see the terms and budgets seem healthy.

It is important that the company still has $49.1 million cash, which gives a financial foundation to end 2025 without needing funding.

All in all, Ur-Energy after long waiting time showed that they can generate revenues. This is however not the case of turnaround just yet.

Risks

Even though Ur-Energy showed the first real sales, I still see some issues and instability risk. They are going from development to production phase, but financials have some holes in them.

Let’s start with losses which are still big. In the second quarter, they have experienced $21 million net loss, and in a half year more than $31 million. This means, that even when revenue started to show up on income statement, operational costs are still outrun it. At this moment, I don’t see them becoming profitable any time soon.

Another serious question is regarding the uranium derivative position, which in balance sheet shows almost $16 million loss. This is market to market obligation, which is directly related to uranium price. If the price of uranium goes down, the company would be obligated to cover the difference. This debt term is until November 2025, it seems like a short term, but this might become a pressure point.

Cash flows are showing problems as well. In first half they have burned through $9 million in operating cash flow and another $8.9 million was invested into projects and capital expenditure. Yes, in July the company had $49.1 million in cash, but this pillow might run out.

Another risk is margins. The contract sales price is $61.56/lb and in the second quarter production cost is $42.8/lb. So, as I mentioned, this leaves with a gross margin of about 18.73/lb, healthy indeed, but not good enough once capital costs are included. If the price of uranium goes down below $60 in spot market, the company would either experience negative gross margin. This could leave the company in unhedged production as well.

Main risk, which I don’t like at all is capital attraction. Even if they have cash today, without additional revenues or actual margin improvement, the future might bring us to new share issuance or debt. This means that shareholder value would go down and the interest rates would give additional pressure. But as I said, this is speculation, that this might happen, currently I don’t see any signs of it coming.

Catalysts

I have said many negative things, let’s move towards the positives. In short-term I see some very good catalysts that would change the situation.

First – production at Lost Creek project. Q2 as I mentioned packed 112 thousand pounds of uranium, which was 35% more than in Q1. The yearly plan is nearing the contracted 400 thousand. If they succeed in this and let’s say “maybe” go above, this excess could be sold in spot market which would mean additional revenue without big costs.

Second – Shirley Basin. The project is moving according to plan. As I said they have built operational building and so on. The start is planned in early 2026, and management expect the unit cost to be at $24.4/lb, which would be even more effective than Lost Creek, so with this project I expect margins to improve significantly.

Third is macroeconomics. US has issued new Nuclear Fuel Security Act with $2.7 billion budget for local uranium producers. At the same time, they have banned LEU import from Russia. Geopolitical tensions in Kazakhstan and Nigeria making tensions on supply as well. It means that US producers (Ur-Energy as well) will have strong demand from government and from private buyers.

The contracts are good as well, the company has eight long term contracts (for 6 million pounds). If deliveries are going to be timely without any issues, this would guarantee stable revenues and stronger reputation as trustworthy supplier.

Summary

Ur-Energy after long wait started showing real results – the sales happened, Lost Creek production is growing, Shirley Basin project is moving according to plan. This is first signal that they are going from promises to real operations phase. But financial side is not yet in order: the losses are still significant, and the derivative debt is making some pressure (and to be honest this is the main red flag I am afraid of).

Positive thing is that US macro environment is becoming more and more suitable for local uranium producers: due to government support, due geopolitical risks in the world. Ur-Energy here can use their situation.

All in all, while losses are not easing and margins stay stagnated, this stock is still risky. I believe, this is the story about potential, rather than actual stable profit. Because of that, I am still maintaining HOLD, I will be waiting for few more quarters to see if production becomes financial stability.

data.title: Expanding The Platform: Intel Joins Nvidia's AI Infrastructure Play
data.description:
In following developments in advanced computing power, AI and how it becomes translated into the economy, announcements such as the Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) partnership warrant attention. The major takeaway was that Nvidia advanced its products and services to include Intel inside the tent. For data center customers, hyperscalers and enterprise, Intel will build Nvidia custom x86 CPUs that Nvidia will integrate into its AI infrastructure platforms. On the press call Sept. 18, Nvidia's Jensen Huang said, "Together, our companies will build custom Intel x86 CPUs for Nvidia's AI infrastructure platforms, our data center platforms, bringing x86 into Nvidia's NVLink ecosystem." His nuance and emphasis are important because many things flow from it.

However, important announcements by Nvidia matter because of who they bring along, Intel in this case. Intel’s stock rose 25% within a couple of hours the day of the announcement. Now comes the work. Both CEOs conveyed respectful and optimistic outlooks. They left politics and competitor digs out of the conversation. Refreshingly, they focused on what mattered, and the word “historic” was used often.

The Verge immediately wrote about Nvidia and Intel working together to connect their architectures via Nvidia’s NVLink communications link system used in data centers to link GPUs together. Huang is cited: “Intel will build Nvidia-custom x86 CPUs that Nvidia will integrate into its AI infrastructure platforms and offer to the market.” My understanding is the offering allows more optionality as firms advance their AI workloads.

“This collaboration tightly couples Nvidia’s AI and accelerated computing stack with Intel’s CPUs and the vast x86 ecosystem — a fusion of two world-class platforms,” says Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang. He noted that the CPU data center segment, the fastest growing segment, is “along the lines of $30 billion a year or so.” He further states:

(Our interest) for whatever general purpose computing platform that has market reach, we would like to be able to accelerate (it) to its fullest capability. We have the benefit of partnering with Intel on a CPU platform that unquestionably has the largest enterprise industrial space cloud consumer footprint of any CPU in the world… We're going to revolutionize this general purpose computing platform by adding and fusing the Nvidia accelerated computing and AI computing architectures.”

They expect to advance next era computing, and specifically in the data center and PC markets. In total, a $50 billion opportunity was cited. For data centers, Huang called their goal “creating rack-scale AI supercomputers.”

The makings and changes of the digital infrastructure market and its developments are a focal area for me. Developments in the AI infrastructure tech stack are an influence, though not the only one. The announcement also signifies that what is being built around AI technologies and digitalization are multifaceted and broadening.

The new PC market with the Nvidia-Intel partnership is further expansion and diffusion of products for consumers and business. The idea is bringing AI computing closer to users, with Nvidia’s GPU chiplets a significant contribution to Intel’s products. Both leaders cited the annual 150 million notebook market. Huang addresses more nuance:

Nvidia's GPU technology is based on TSMC's foundry. And this is one of the extraordinary things that you can do connecting Nvidia's GPU chiplet with Intel's CPUs in a multi-technology packaging capability and multi-process packaging technology. It's really a fabulous way of mixing and matching technology. It’s one of the reasons why we're going to be able to innovate so quickly and build these incredibly complex systems and deliver it...”

Zooming out, in a conversation with a semiconductor expert, when asked about competition and custom chip trends, he noted that Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Tesla (TSLA) and others are developing ASICs (application-specific integrated circuits) with Broadcom (AVGO) partnerships, for example. Custom chips can be potentially more energy efficient and cost effective for workloads specific to their purposes. The idea of mix and match is apparent in this context as well. There are no longer only giant firms at the top of the food chain. The food groups are expanding, some organically, and some new pairings are juxtaposed. Supply chains are reworking forward and backward.

(See backgrounder video: Vibing Out: Latest Trends of AI, Chips and Transformations Afoot)

While the world watches Nvidia’s moves in AI-accelerated computing, other competitors are carving the market up and out, too. For these segments, I leave it to my semiconductor ETF (SMH) and the Spear Alpha ETF (SPRX) for representation, plus some Oracle (ORCL), Broadcom, and others via (TDIV). Plus, broader indices and funds get a slice of the action. The market caps are just staggering these days. But the common denominator is the advancing of technology, with AI and its brand of accelerated compute moving the curve ahead.

data.title: Manchester United: Buy The Team Disappointment
data.description:
The public markets continue to not be nice to Manchester United plc (NYSE:MANU). The storied English Premier League club has struggled to produce a championship-worthy team the last few years, but the franchise value is still estimated at a far higher level. My investment thesis remains ultra bullish on the stock, as sports franchise values continue to rise and the stock market remains disconnected.

Struggles Persist

Man U just reported FQ4'25 results with revenues up 15.4% to hit £164.1 million. The sports franchise reported record results, but total FY25 revenues were only up a fraction at 0.7% to reach £666.5 million.

The business reported a solid adjusted EBITDA of £37.5 million, nearly double the £19.3 million reported last FQ4. The yearly adjusted EBITDA is up ~24% to £182.8 million after the company spent the last few years restructuring the business.

Man U reported some encouraging trends, with commercial and matchday revenues up. The soccer team was successful in driving higher revenue for general matches due in part to more home matches, but the biggest issue is the broadcasting segment.

Broadcasting revenue for FY24 was only £172.9 million, a decrease of £48.9 million, or 22.0%, over the prior year. The primary problem is the men's first team participating in the UEFA Europa League tournament compared to the UEFA Champions League in the prior year, in addition to finishing 15th in the EPL last season.

After 4 weeks into the new EPL season, Man U is off to another horrible start. The club only has 4 points and sits in 14th place after a 1-1-2 start, which includes an embarrassing 3-0 loss to crosstown rival Manchester City.

The promising part is that management has improved corporate performance, leading to stronger financials. The next step is returning the men's club to historical championship-level performance, thereby driving the broadcasting revenues to prior levels, if not higher.

Man U guided to FY26 revenue of £640 million to £660 million and adjusted EBITDA guidance of £180 million to £200 million. The revenue guidance forecasts a further slump from last year, while adjusted EBITDA could rise due to cost controls.

Sports Franchise Disconnect

Despite the struggles on the field, Man U remains one of the most valuable soccer franchises in the world. The latest Forbes report estimates the value at $6.6 billion, up just 1% from last year.

CNBC has a list placing the value of Man U at $6 billion, still behind Real Madrid at $6.7 billion. The one negative is that soccer team values aren't rising much in the last year.

Real Madrid is now the most valuable franchise, with revenues topping $1.1 billion in the last year. The La Liga team offers signs of where Man U can push with a championship team playing in the later stages of the UEFA Champions League in the future.

Man U only has a market cap in the $2.6 billion range after falling back toward the $15 range of the last several years. Historically, Man U has traded far below the listed franchise value.

Sir Jim Ratcliffe made a large investment last year to help improve the prospects of the franchise, yet the stock is back to the lows now. The franchise is working on a new stadium to return Man U to its prior glory. The proposed 100,000-seat stadium hopes to maintain the feel of the iconic Old Trafford while providing the modern amenities of newer stadiums, allowing the club to host additional events, such as NFL games.

The stock only trades at 3x sales despite the vanity valuations usually assigned to professional sports teams. Even more amazing, Man U trades at just 13x the top-end adjusted EBITDA targets, giving retail investors access to a premium sports franchise at a reasonable price.

The company has net debt of ~$520 million. Even if the stock valuation doubled to $5.2 billion, the enterprise value would still sit below $6.0 billion and the estimated franchise value for just the men's first team.

The biggest risk for Man U is failing to remain in the EPL by ending up in the bottom 3 teams for the 25/26 season. A regulation to the EFL Championship league would crush revenues and likely send the stock lower.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Man U continues to provide an opportunity to own a premier sports franchise at a discounted valuation. The stock actually trades more like a value play, but investors face the reality that Man U could trade at these lower valuations for an extended period.

data.title: U.S. Natural Gas Prices Under Pressure
data.description:
By Ewa Manthey, Warren Patterson

Energy - US natural gas storage rises

Oil prices drifted lower for a third straight session in the early trading session today, following the latest comments from President Trump who called for lower oil prices to pressure Moscow to end the war in Ukraine. Trump said the war would end “if the price of oil comes down,” and repeated calls for countries to stop buying fuel from the OPEC+ member. On the other hand, upside risks for oil continue following the recent attacks on Russian energy infrastructure by Ukraine.

Insights Global data shows that refined product inventories in the Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp (ARA) region decreased by 239kt week-on-week to 5.92mt for the week ending September 18, 2025. The fall was largely driven by naphtha and fuel oil inventories declining by 120kt and 52kt to 547kt and 986kt, respectively. Similarly, gasoline stocks also declined marginally by 18kt week-on-week to 1.16mt, while gasoil stocks fell by 11kt WoW to 2.2mt over the reporting week.

In Singapore, onshore refined product stocks fell by a little over 1m barrels for a second straight session to 49.5m barrels over the reporting week. Residual fuel inventories and middle distillate stocks fell by 1.1m barrels and 144k barrels to 25.4m barrels and 9.7m barrels, respectively. In contrast, light distillate stocks grew marginally by 259k barrels to 14.4mt for the week ending September 17, 2025.

Separately, EIA weekly gas storage data shows that US gas stocks rose by 90Bcf last week, higher than the average market expectations of a build of around 80bcf. This was also well above the five-year average addition of 74Bcf for this time of the year. The bigger-than-average increase was largely due to the forecasts for cooler forecasts for late September. Total gas stockpiles totalled 3.43Tcf as of September 12, which is 6.3% above the five-year average. The front-month Henry Hub contract traded under pressure and hovered around US$2.9/MMBtu in the early trading session today.

European natural gas prices ended higher for a third consecutive session yesterday. TTF futures rose by 1.7% to settle just below EUR33/MWh. Prices remain supported amid rising concerns over Russia’s liquified natural gas phase-out plans. Recent reports suggest that the European Commission is considering options to end imports of Russian liquefied natural gas earlier than the end of 2027, which was the initial plan. Russia supplies more than 10% of the European Union’s gas imports, roughly half of which comes via LNG. Meanwhile, EU storage is now 81% full, down from 93.4% at the same time last year and below the five-year average of 87.6%. The expectations for cooler weather in northwest Europe over the coming week might slow down the fuel injections as the region braces for the start of the heating season.

Metals - LME aluminium inventories rise

LME data shows that total exchange inventories for aluminium rose by 30,125 tonnes (the biggest daily addition since May 22, 2024) to 513,900 tonnes as of yesterday, the highest since March 6, 2025. The majority of the inflows were reported into Malaysia’s Port Klang warehouses. Meanwhile, on-warrant inventories of aluminium rose by 29,650 tonnes to 404,675 tonnes, while cancelled warrants increased slightly by 475 tonnes to 109,225 tonnes for the above-mentioned period.

Recent reports suggest that Ivanhoe Mines (OTCQX:IVPAF) expect medium-term production at the Kamoa-Kakula copper mine to exceed 550kt, down from its earlier target of over 600kt, after flooding caused by seismic activity in May, likely triggered by mining activity. This has forced the company to draw up a new long-term development plan for the mine. Meanwhile, the company aims to issue production guidance for 2026 and 2027 once recovery efforts progress further. As one of the world’s largest copper mines, Kamoa-Kakula has been a key contributor to global supply as other major mines struggle to maintain output.

Agriculture - IGC lowers corn and soybean output estimates

In its recent monthly update, the International Grains Council (IGC) increased its 2025/26 global wheat output forecasts from 811mt to 819mt, while consumption projections increased to 819mt from previous estimates of 816mt. An increase in production resulted in rising global wheat ending stock projections to 270mt from 264mt projected a month earlier. For soybeans, the IGC lowered the 2025/26 production estimates to 429mt, compared to earlier estimates of 430mt. Soybean ending stock estimates fell to 83mt from 85mt, whilst the consumption projections were increased from 430mt to 431mt. Looking at corn, the council left the global corn ending stock estimates unchanged at 294mt. Corn production projections were trimmed to 1,297mt from 1,299mt, whereas consumption estimates increased slightly to 1,286mt from 1,285mt seen in August.

Brazil’s agriculture agency, CONAB, in its first estimates for the 2025/26 season expects soybean production to rise 3.6% year-on-year to a record 177.7mt. Despite pressure on domestic prices and profitability challenges, the crop continues to offer high liquidity and attractive returns, driving a 3.7% YoY increase in harvest area. In contrast, CONAB expects corn production to decline slightly by 1% YoY to 138.3mt in 2025/26, primarily due to a lower yield projection. Meanwhile, the corn harvest is expected to rise by 3.5% YoY, supported by strong domestic demand and rising orders from Asian buyers.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Intel Is A Near-Term Hold, Taiwan Semiconductor Is A Long-Term Buy
data.description:
INTC enjoys support from US government and Nvidia

I last analyzed Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) on July 23 in an article titled "TSMC: Time To Hedge With Options (Rating Downgrade)". The article rated the stock as a buy after considering its implied volatility at that time. As for Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC), my last analysis was published on Aug 5 with an article titled "Intel Q2: A Sober 'State Of The Union' Report." The article provided a review of its FQ2 earnings report (ER) and concluded with a hold rating.

Since then, two material developments have emerged with potential impacts on both companies. First, the Trump Administration has gained a 10% stake in Intel for $11.1 billion. The stake primarily comes from grants to Intel via the CHIPS and Science Act, an act signed by President Biden to foster domestic manufacturing of advanced chips. As a second and more recent development, Nvidia (NVDA) has announced a deal to co-develop PC and data center chips with INTC and also to take a $5B stake in Intel stocks.

These developments, when combined, have triggered large stock price movements for INTC shares, as you can see from the chart below. Given the competitive dynamics between INTC and TSM in the foundry space and NVDA's role as a key customer for TSM, I believe these developments will keep generating long-lasting impacts on both companies beyond the short-term stock price movements. As such, the goal of this article is to perform an analysis of TSM and INTC's long-term fundamentals, and my conclusion is that Intel remains a near-term hold while TSM is a long-term buy.

INTC and TSM: R&D expenses and yield

As high-tech firms in a rapidly evolving sector, R&D investments and effectiveness hold a central role for their long-term prospects in my model. The next figure compares the R&D expenses of INTC and TSM as a percentage of their sales in recent years. As seen, TSM has been maintaining a consistent R&D in recent years of around 6~7% of its sales. In contrast, INTC has been investing more aggressively and has further increased its investments around 2022 in tandem of its foundry initiatives. To wit, INTC has been spending around 20% of revenue on R&D efforts during 2021 and 2022. The ratio rose notably to an average of around 29% as seen since 2023.

Interpretation of the above comparison certainly depends on different investors' perspectives. One possible perspective, a perspective that is in INTC's favor, is that more R&D means heavier emphasis on new product developments. Furthermore, since INTC spends so much more on R&D, the government's support via the CHIPS Act and also its collaboration with NVDA can have a more drastic impact on its further R&D expenses.

However, another aspect, the aspect I place more weight on from past experiences, is the yield from the R&D expenses. These expenses are ultimately inputs (i.e., costs). I tend to focus more on the outputs as measured by the R&D yield shown in the next chart. In this chart, I extended Warren Buffett's $1 test on retained earnings (as detailed in our earlier articles) to R&D expenses. In a nutshell, the chart shows how many extra dollars of organic cash flow were generated per $1 of R&D expenses for both companies. To smooth the curves, I took a 3-year running average, assuming an average 3-year R&D cycle. By this measure, you can see the R&D yield for TSM has been far superior to INTC. In recent years, the average R&D yield for TSM is around $7.99 and approaches $10 as of the latest quarter. In contrast, the R&D yield for INTC has been averaging around $2.05 only, and its R&D yield has been under pressure and falling below the long-term average in recent quarters.

INTC and TSM: Profitability metrics

Another long-term fundamental metric that I pay special attention to is ROCE (return on capital employed). Judging by this metric, I also see a far stabler and wider economic moat for TSM. To be more explicit, the chart below compares the ROCE of INTC and TSM over recent quarters since the past few years since Q4 of 2022. In this comparison, I included the following items as capital actually employed in their operations: A) Working capital (including payables, receivables, inventory), B) Gross Property, Plant, and Equipment, and C) Research and development expenses are also capitalized. Note that cash and equivalents were excluded.

As seen, TSM was able to maintain an overall consistent ROCE of an average of 49%, and its ROCE in recent quarters has been trending up and above its historical average. In contrast, INTC's ROCE has been consistently lower, with an average of about 36 in the period. Moreover, the company's earnings and ROCE have become quite volatile lately, so I had to perform a running average on its data before plotting them. Otherwise, its ROCE would be negative for several of the quarters and would further drag down the average.

Other risks and final thoughts

The root cause for TSM's superior ROCE lies in its concentration on fabrication and the wide technological lead on this crucial front. For example, TSM had already matured the 7 nm technology back in 2018 and the 5 nm process in 2020. Per the recent TSMC 2025 Technical Symposium, the company has further extended its roadmap to 2028, as you can see from the screenshot below, with new technologies like N3C and A14. Such a technological lead positions TSM well for long-term growth in futuristic areas like AI, humanoid robot, and robotaxi applications.

As an additional positive for TSM, its current P/E is also significantly lower than INTC's P/E. For the current fiscal year (FY1), Intel's P/E is an extremely high 211 as seen, whereas TSM's is a much more reasonable 26.83x. The contrast diminishes in FY2 and FY3 with Intel's valuation projected to a P/E ratio of 38.1x and 19.8x respectively.

There are certainly positives for Intel stock too. After all, I am rating the stock as a hold. Fundamentally, Intel operates as an integrated device manufacturer (IDM) in my mind. It designs, manufactures, and also markets its own chips. This vertical integration provides several key advantages over TSM, a pure-play foundry that manufactures chips for other companies, in terms of cost control, intellectual property, and also concentration exposure to any single customer. Secondly, as the recent developments have demonstrated, the design and manufacturing of advanced chips are considered a matter of national importance. Thus, investors certainly need to consider the geopolitical risks when evaluating both companies.

All told, this comparison of INTC and TSM is triggered by the US government's and also NVDA's stake in INTC. These developments have drastically changed the near-term prospects of INTC, as reflected in its stock price movements lately. The purpose of this article is to look past these near-term impacts and evaluate the long-term return potential. This evaluation has led me to keep seeing a more favorable return/risk ratio for TSM stock compared to INTC.

data.title: Smithfield: A Leader In Packaged Pork Products At A Deep Value Price
data.description:
Pork is the most consumed meat in the world, representing 36% of all meat consumed. It’s a staple of diets worldwide, particularly in China, Europe, and the US, and could grow in diet share thanks to a few tailwinds. For one, despite being classified as red meat, compared to beef, some studies have shown no harmful effects of pork consumption on cardiovascular risk factors. So pork consumption could increase as a result of consumers shifting to a healthier diet. Second, with beef (and poultry) prices breaking out of late, there is potential value in pork, which has not experienced such a dramatic price increase.

At 25% of the American diet share (compared to 50% for poultry and 25% for beef), there is room for the market share of pork in the U.S. to grow towards the global average of 36% as consumers reach for cheaper and/or healthier options at the grocery store.

Why Smithfield?

Smithfield (NASDAQ:SFD) is an old company that recently made its return to the public markets via a Nasdaq IPO on January 28th (though few noticed or took any interest). Back in 2013, Smithfield was taken private by WH Group for $4.7B. At the time, Smithfield was mainly known as a hog producer. But since going private, they have adopted several efficiency measures, started to reduce their reliance on hog raising, invested in manufacturing capacity, and sharpened their focus on higher-margin packaged meats.

Today’s Smithfield is a better company than 2013’s Smithfield, and you can invest in today’s Smithfield at a highly attractive valuation, lower than peers like Hormel (HRL) and Tyson Foods (TSN), and on par with 2013’s Smithfield. This low multiple does not take into account the transformation that has been occurring at the company over the last 10 years. Today’s Smithfield has a healthy balance sheet, providing flexibility to invest in production and strategic acquisitions, and pays a $1 dividend that is well covered by free cash flow and is likely to grow over time. Plus, insiders, including the CEO, chairman, and CFO, have been purchasing shares.

Smithfield’s Leading Brands

Smithfield is the largest pork processor in the U.S., and the second largest in terms of packaged meat sales by volume, with products across 25 categories. Their portfolio consists of leading brands like Smithfield, Eckrich, and Nathan’s Famous (64% of sales combined), and private label offerings across a range of price points (36% of sales). This balance gives them the opportunity to capture consumer demand in any economic environment (See Prospectus for more information). Smithfield brands are well-regarded—the company won the 2022 BrandSpark Most Trusted Award for pork products, and the 2025 International Foodservice Distributors Association Distributor’s Choice Award.

You can find Smithfield products in all of the top 10 national grocery retailers, including Walmart, Sam’s Club, Costco, Kroger, and Albertsons. And, if you like fast food, there’s a good chance you’ve eaten Smithfield products, because they serve 70% of the top 50 chains, including McDonald’s, Subway, and IHOP.

Products include bacon, sausage, hot dogs, deli, dry sausage (like pepperoni), and ham products, covering breakfast, lunch, and dinner – a unique feature of pork. Smithfield has a strong position in each of these subcategories, with #1 or #2 positions in bacon, sausages, deli meat, and smoked ham.

Source: January 29, 2025 Prospectus.

The Growth Opportunity

Smithfield is actively investing in its packaged meats business, improving efficiency and developing new, high-margin packaged products such as dried sausages and charcuterie. They are shifting away from traditional products, like holiday hams (low-margin hunks of meat). At the same time, they are shrinking the size of their hog production business, which is susceptible to huge swings in profit due to volatile commodity prices. For example, the company recently transferred part of its hog farming operations to Murphy Family Ventures LLC, and sold some farms in Utah and Missouri. These efforts will provide strong cost visibility, reduce the volatility of earnings, and boost overall margins. They are targeting a reduction to 30% internal hog production from around 50% today, which should still be sufficient to meet internal meat processing needs and retain the benefits of vertical integration. Overall margins have been steadily increasing as the company executes this strategy, but this transformation is still not reflected in the price of SFD shares.

Fortunately, the hog production segment of the business has been doing well of late. And, with ongoing favourable market conditions – strong pork prices combined with feed prices that have moderated significantly – this segment should continue to perform well in the near term.

Going forward, earnings growth will come from ongoing product mix improvements, volume growth, and innovation. For example, last year they expanded dry sausage capacity by 50M lbs to grow in this higher-margin product category, which increased 37% in sales between 2019 and 2024. This is a subcategory in which they have an 11% market share despite having the #2 position (see image above), so there’s plenty of room to take share. They’re also taking steps to increase sales of lunch meat through the Smithfield Prime Fresh premium brand.

The share of the packaged meats segment, already 58% of total sales, should continue to grow. This high-margin segment has grown profit at an impressive 9.8% CAGR over the past 10 years. With a roughly 20% overall market share, there remains space for Smithfield to continue to take share. In addition, as previously discussed, pork could take share from beef, as the cattle shortage in the U.S., which is keeping beef prices elevated, is not showing any signs of reversing. Pork consumption is also supported by the growing popularity in the U.S. of Asian and Mexican cuisines, which feature a lot of pork-based dishes. Referencing several forecasts from market research companies, Smithfield expects its pork product end markets to grow ~4% over the next 5 years.

With these tailwinds, the company is in a good position to continue to outperform, beat expectations, and raise guidance.

Valuation

Tyson Foods is probably the best comp for Smithfield. Even though the business has not done well recently, TSN trades at a forward earnings multiple of 14x, which is about average for the stock. Packaged food sales represent less than 20% of Tyson’s total sales ($2.5B of $13.9B total sales), with the rest coming from lower-margin meat sales.

Despite the fact that Smithfield has a higher proportion of sales coming from packaged meats, about 59%, SFD shares trade for just 10x forward earnings.

Pilgrim’s Pride (PPC) is optically cheap at 8.7x forward EPS, but the business is mostly focused on fresh meat sales, with prepared products accounting for 26% of revenues.

Hormel generates most of its revenues from packaged foods, and HRL stock trades at 17x forward earnings, which is on the low end of its historical range.

So, in light of these comps, what’s a reasonable target multiple for SFD? Certainly, multiple matching TSN’s would be reasonable, though you could also argue for a higher multiple, especially as Smithfield makes continued progress on improving its revenue mix towards higher-margin packaged meats.

At $2.39 consensus EPS for 2026 and a 14x multiple, equivalent to TSN, SFD shares would trade at $33.5 – a ~40% increase over today’s price.

Risks

Smithfield is a controlled company, with Hong Kong-based WH Group retaining about 87% ownership.

WH Group is led by 84-year-old Chair Wan Long. He has been involved with the company since the beginning, dropping out of high school and climbing the corporate ranks to the top, eventually spearheading an IPO in 1998 for the Shuanghui Group, which was renamed to WH Group in 2014.

Smithfield initially offered to sell 17.4M shares for $23-27 a share, but at the IPO, WH Group and Smithfield sold 13 million shares each at $20 a share, raising $522M. Mr. Long personally participated in the IPO, purchasing $40M of SFD stock and signaling that he believes the stock is undervalued.

A secondary offering by WH Group took place on September 8th, with 22.5M shares offered at $23.25 a share. Again, Mr. Long participated, buying an additional 1.8M shares. Smithfield executives, including the CFO, also participated in both the IPO and the secondary offering.

While the WH Group overhang scares off some investors, it’s encouraging to see insiders, including the WH Group chairman, absorb the shares. Plus, spinning off Smithfield seems to have been a rational decision and has been a success, so far, for WH Group. The price of SFD stock is up about 25% since the IPO, while WH Group stock is up around 50%.

Investors appear to be accepting the notion that a U.S.-market IPO for Smithfield is the best way to unlock value for both companies. WH Group has unlocked capital, while Smithfield has emerged with a clean balance sheet and better access to U.S. capital markets. Both companies now have separate boards of directors, and with Smithfield focused on the US and Mexico, and WH Group on China and Europe, the two groups no longer have to compete for capital under a single entity.

As a majority owner of SFD, with its ownership representing more than half of WH Group’s market cap, it simply makes no sense for WH Group to take actions that push the price of SFD stock down. So far, they are behaving like sound managers of the business. The secondary offering, which caused the stock price to fall almost 10%, has presented patient investors with a nice entry opportunity.

If WH Group does want to exit its stake completely, it will take a long time to do so. The good news is, with ample free cash flow and not much leverage, Smithfield should be able to steadily buy back shares from WH Group.

Smithfield is recovering from a terrible period in 2023, which featured rising feed costs and decreasing hog prices, causing operating profits to drop ~600M YoY. If this were to happen again, it would temporarily hurt the company’s profitability. However, recall that they are actively reducing the scale of their hog production business, so the impact should be lower than in the past.

Finally, a brief note on tariffs. Smithfield exports to over 30 countries, including China. Most of these exports are fresh pork products, and exports to China account for only about 2% of total sales. China has had tariffs on U.S. pork products in place for many years now, so while Trump’s trade war has made the situation worse, it’s not a new threat for Smithfield.

data.title: Cheniere Energy: Effects Of Power Of Siberia 2 On US LNG Exports Are Misunderstood
data.description:
Investment thesis: As the Power of Siberia 2 pipeline deal news surfaced, the most immediate reaction was to assume that this is a net negative for America's LNG exporters, such as Cheniere (NYSE:LNG). Putting aside the fact that it will be many years before the pipeline becomes operational, assuming that it will become operational at all, given that not every aspect of the deal is finalized yet, there is actually some potential upside for America's LNG exporters if this project does materialize. Among other things, it will help lock in America's dominance in the European market, giving it more pricing power, as Europe loses the ability to shift to alternatives if the price of natural gas is not competitive. Cheniere is currently a hold for me, but I am looking for a better entry point as a long-term investment, given that I see continued demand growth for LNG globally, even with the return of more Russian gas on the global markets.

Continued revenue & earnings growth, while maintaining a healthy profit margin, were in part achieved thanks to higher LNG prices.

For the first half of the year, Cheniere saw a 43% increase in net income to $1.98 billion and a 35% increase in revenues to $10.1 billion compared to the same period in 2024. Most of the increase came on the back of more favorable LNG prices, since its LNG sales volumes increased by a more modest rate of under 1% for the period. The improved financial performance is, therefore, arguably a reflection of LNG price volatility that can go either way. The fact that earnings increased at a faster pace than revenues suggests that some gains in profitability have been achieved along the way, which can be seen as perhaps the positive takeaway from these results. There are, of course, reasons to expect that LNG prices will not decline to last year's levels, which I shall discuss in the last section of the article.

Other results of note include the company's long-term debt situation, which stands at $22 billion, down just slightly from $22.5 billion at the start of the year. Interest expenses came in at $466 million in the first half of 2025. Interest expenses as a percent of revenues came in at 4.6%, which is in the danger zone from my perspective. I tend to worry about the financial health of companies that spend around 5% or higher of their revenues on servicing their debt.

The Power of Siberia 2 deal and Cheniere's exposure to any ramifications for the global market.

The news of the signed deal between Russia & China to build the Power of Siberia 2 pipeline was greeted as a globally significant event, with geopolitical, economic, and sectoral ramifications for the energy industry, given the large volumes involved of 50 Bcm/year. It remains to be seen if it actually goes ahead, with pricing disputes reportedly yet to be resolved. As of right now, it seems that Russia will have to sell the gas significantly below the price levels it would fetch on the European markets to clinch the final go-ahead for the project.

One of the prominent questions asked and speculations offered is the effect this will have on the longer-term prospects for US LNG exports.

As we can see, Europe, not Asia, is Cheniere's main market by far, since the all-out war in Ukraine started in 2022, which is also the case with most US LNG exporters. From this perspective, there is no direct impact on Cheniere's exports.

With the massive 50 Bcm/year volume, in addition to another 50 billion Bcm that is already flowing from Russia to China or is currently being planned, one can argue that this threatens global LNG demand, affecting the entire market as Russia's natural gas exports are set to return to levels we saw before the 2022 invasion of Ukraine once this pipeline will be in place. This may be true, but there is also continued global natural gas demand growth to consider, with demand rising by more than 50 Bcm/year currently. Some countries are seeing a decline in output capacity, such as Norway, for instance, where Europe's largest natural gas supplier is set to see output decline by 14 Bcm between 2024 and 2030.

The return of another 50 Bcms of Russian gas to the global market is a significant factor, with significant implications for the industry and individual companies. Once the pipeline deal is finalized and a start date for deliveries through the pipeline is provided, LNG companies should consider readjusting their plans for capacity expansion accordingly.

As we can see, the EIA is expecting flat US natural gas output going forward. In fact, the current quarter might see the highest average daily production at 107.2 Bcf/day, with production coming in slightly lower every quarter thereafter, at least until the end of 2026. Keeping this in mind, LNG companies might have other reasons aside from the China-Russia pipeline deal to consider whether they should invest in further LNG export capacities from this point forward.

Cheniere is engaged in expanding its Sabine Pass plant, with an intent to add as many as three more Trains to the six that are already operational. Its Corpus Cristi site is still in the middle of ramping up output, with the last Trains set to come online by the end of next year. If the EIA forecast for natural gas output to stagnate has merit, it is a sign of what is to come beyond the end of next year. Adding to it the uncertainty caused by the prospects of Russia regaining its pre-2022 place in the natural gas export markets, perhaps there are some valid questions about Cheniere's strategy of further increasing its LNG export capacity in the United States.

Looking at its expansion plans, versus its current P/E ratio at 15, currently on a forward basis, the company's continued growth trajectory, versus its P/E relative to other energy companies, is what makes it an interesting potential investment opportunity.

Compared with other energy companies, such as Shell (SHEL), the most dominant LNG player currently, which is trading at a forward P/E of just over 11, its P/E ratio is just slightly higher, even though it still has ample room to grow, while Shell is a mature, integrated energy company with few growth prospects. Of course, there are reasons to discount Cheniere's valuation, such as its high debt servicing costs, approaching 5% of revenues, whereas Shell spent 1.6% on servicing its debt in the first half of this year. There is also the dividend, which in the case of Shell, is almost 4%, compared with Cheniere's at less than 1%. At the same time, Cheniere's business volume could grow about 50% in the next few years based on existing expansion projects, with possibly more growth beyond, which makes it a growth stock that is currently trading more like a mature energy company.

Investment implications:

Cheniere's recent financial results have been impressive, but it has to be recognized that the main driver of those results has been the rise in LNG prices, especially on the European market.

It should be noted that natural gas prices in Europe have been trending lower in the past few months, despite the precarious inventory situation, relative to recent years; therefore, the current quarter might not be as impressive for Cheniere in terms of revenues and profits. A potential disappointment in its Q3 report might provide for a better buying opportunity.

The longer-term challenges for Cheniere do include the prospects of Russia and others, such as Iran, ramping up natural gas exports, but Europe, its main market currently and probably for the foreseeable future, is unlikely to buy much, if any gas from those countries, with plans in place to eliminate Russian energy from the European market. If anything, there is a positive aspect for Cheniere and its pricing power in the European market, because it eliminates the opportunity for Europe to pivot politically and return to importing significant amounts of cheaper pipeline gas from Russia, which used to be the price setter in the European market.

As for the issue of stagnating US natural gas output, perhaps making Cheniere's continued capacity expansion unwise, we have to take a closer look at how this would play out in terms of the US natural gas market. Stagnated output would lead to a rise in natural gas prices, which would then encourage more drilling, potentially providing more natural gas. It would also lead to lower domestic demand due to substitution effects. At some price point, utilities will switch to burning coal, as long as permitted by government regulations. In other words, Cheniere will probably have to pay a higher price for its natural gas purchases, but it will most likely be able to fully utilize its future export capacity.

Summing it up, I do see continued demand growth for LNG, and Cheniere will be able to utilize its investments in extra capacity by securing the necessary volumes of natural gas, even if it will have to pay more. Even with America's natural gas output stagnating, LNG exports can still increase as long as market forces are allowed to dictate flows. Cheniere's stock price is currently about 10% lower than its all-time high, and I do believe that it has some more potential downside on weaker Q3 results relative to the financial results we saw in the first half of the year. In the aftermath of its next quarterly report, there will probably be a better opportunity to invest, which is why I have it as a hold for now.

  • Risks to my thesis.

The main risk to my hold position is that the current situation with Europe's natural gas inventories can lead to a sizable LNG price surge this winter.

As we can see, inventories are below the five-year average and a sizable 14% below last year's levels, which also means that LNG prices are unlikely to retreat from current levels; in fact, the opposite might be the case. It would not take much of a weather-related event that causes an increase in natural gas demand in Europe to trigger a price spike. While there is a risk of missing out by waiting for Cheniere's Q3 results to come out, I also think that a European natural gas price spike is unlikely to happen by then. Market complacency has been evident, with little reaction to inventories being under the five-year average throughout 2025. I expect this complacency to continue, even if there will be some early signs of trouble for Europe's energy situation this winter.

  • Cheniere's current stock price is boosted by higher Y-O-Y LNG prices, which will not be the case for the rest of the year.

Aside from the debt-servicing situation that I find to be somewhat worrying, in terms of the proportion of revenues it takes to pay the yields, Cheniere has the financial profile of a company that is about to hit maturity, with a healthy financial situation overall, and good prospects for future performance. That performance is in large part underpinned by decent global LNG market prospects for the long term. This makes Cheniere a good long-term investment prospect; however, its financial performance metrics are likely to look somewhat worse after its Q3 results come out, which is why I intend to wait for now, even though there is some risk that waiting might mean an opportunity to invest will be missed.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bond Brief: Spreads Are Tight, But Yields Are (Still) Alright
data.description:
By Gene Tannuzzo, CFA & Ed Al-Hussainy

It’s been quite a year so far for the bond market. While fundamentals remain strong, spreads are historically expensive, the labor market is in flux, and now the Federal Reserve (Fed) has started cutting rates. In this third quarter 2025 fixed-income update, we’ll look at how we’re adjusting portfolios to deliver value for investors in the current environment.

Fundamentals drown out political noise

Despite a turbulent start to 2025 and plenty of headlines, bond markets have shown impressive staying power, moving swiftly past the tariff turmoil following Liberation Day to refocus on fundamentals.

One of the most significant macroeconomic developments this quarter is the muted impact of tariffs on inflation. While tariffs initially sparked concern about rising prices, the data so far does not show seismic shocks to consumer prices. That’s largely because the U.S. economy is diversified and more domestically focused — roughly one-third of the personal consumption expenditure (PCE) basket consists of goods, and only one-fourth of those goods are imported. That means that just about 8% of the PCE basket is directly affected by tariffs. And with little in the way of excess (pent-up) demand, this insularity has helped keep inflationary pressure from trade disruption in check — for now.

At the same time, fiscal policy — in the form of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act — is also providing some welcome front-loaded, albeit limited, stimulus. These are all reasons we’re optimistic.

The caution in our optimism is the labor market, which remains the biggest conundrum as we head into year-end. Hiring has decelerated, but unemployment rates haven’t budged, reflecting a sharp slowdown in immigration. We're flying at a low altitude, so the margin of error for policymakers and investors is pretty small. The downside risk to the labor market has motivated the Fed to cut rates.

Moving from offense to defense in portfolios

These changes in fiscal and monetary policy have led to meaningful shifts in portfolio positioning heading into the third quarter. We took an offensive approach with credit during the April sell-off, when significant dislocations in bond markets and a widening of spreads created buying opportunities. We made significant adjustments to capitalize on these dislocations and have since taken profits on many of those positions. With credit no longer attractively priced, we're shifting to a defensive stance.

Historically tight credit spreads reflect continued corporate and economic strength. At the same time, yields across investment-grade and high-yield sectors remain compelling — investment-grade bonds are yielding around 5%, while high-yield bonds are closer to 7%. That’s a far cry from just a few years ago, when investment grade and high yield were yielding 1.5% and 3.5%, respectively.

What does this mean for bond investors? This is an environment that rewards careful credit selection and a focus on shorter duration, where investors can earn attractive coupons while managing risk. Within high yield, that means avoiding companies with unsustainable capital structures. In investment grade, it’s about relative value — with the corporate index and mortgage index both yielding 4.9%, the latter is implicitly guaranteed by the U.S. government.

Debt is financing the AI investment boom

Artificial intelligence (AI), once considered an equity story, now has the potential to drive significant issuance in fixed income. Technology and utility companies are borrowing to fund data center expansion and energy infrastructure for AI applications. If priced and structured correctly, these new opportunities could be compelling additions to portfolios.

Bonds remain an important diversifier

Bonds continue to prove their worth as portfolio diversifiers. The reason they matter for multi-asset portfolios is that they tend to move in the opposite direction to stocks, particularly in a significant risk drawdown. In a world where global equities have surged across regions and sectors, bonds are well positioned to outperform if equities turn. Moreover, amid concerns about fiscal policy and monetary policy credibility in the U.S., other developed market sovereign debt (such as Germany, France and the U.K.) can be a diversifier in a global bond portfolio. In short, diversifying your safe haven is a smart move in fixed income.

The bottom line

The bond market continues to move in response to policy shocks, but strong fundamentals have been — and will continue to be — a meaningful buffer. Investors should look past the headlines, focus on fundamentals and use bonds as strategic diversifiers. With attractive yields, tight spreads and falling rates, the playbook is clear: Stay selective and stay invested.

© 2016-2025 Columbia Threadneedle. All rights reserved.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund carefully before investing. To learn more about this and other important information about each fund, download a free prospectus. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

data.title: How To Gain Index Traction In Defense
data.description:
By Zoe Morrison, Senior Analyst, Equity Index Product

Geopolitical instability is rising, and there are widespread demands to increase defence spending, as well as a raft of recently announced national commitments to do so. Equity investors are paying close attention to this trend and share prices are moving as a result.

In this FTSE Russell Insight, we look at the indices from our range that best enable investors to express a market view on the burgeoning defence and aerospace sector.

Defence spending is on the rise

Last year saw a large acceleration in global defence spending, reflecting perceptions of growing geopolitical instability: according to the International Institute for Strategic Studies[1], global defence spending reached US$2.46trn in 2024, a big jump from US$2.24trn in the previous year.

This 7.4% inflation-adjusted increase outpaced the real-terms rises seen in 2023 and 2022 (6.5% and 3.5%, respectively).

Meanwhile, the Trump Administration has intensified the conversation with European NATO allies about defence funding, suggesting an increased allocation of national budgets to the military.

In January 2025, President Trump said that the minimum level of expenditure for NATO members should increase to 5% of gross domestic product (GDP)[2]. Currently, only Poland, Latvia, Estonia, Greece and the US spend more than 3% of their GDP on defence[3] - a significant shortfall by comparison with Trump’s stated goal.

Recent outperformance in Europe and the US

How can investors best express a sector-wide view on aerospace and defence stocks? The Aerospace and Defense sector falls within the Industrials Industry under the Industry Classification Benchmark (ICB), which is operated and maintained by FTSE Russell.

The Aerospace and Defense sector includes the manufacturers, assemblers and distributors of aircraft and aircraft parts that are used in commercial or private air transport. It also includes companies procuring components and equipment for the defence industry (including military aircraft, radar equipment and weapons).

However, the sector excludes manufacturers of communications satellites. These belong to the telecommunications equipment subsector (which lives within a separate ICB Industry, Telecommunications).

Recent outperformance in Europe and the US

The recent pressure on European governments to boost their military spending has led to significant gains in both European and US aerospace and defence stocks: reflecting the recent acceleration of defence spending on both sides of the Atlantic Ocean, both the FTSE Europe All Cap Aerospace and Defense index and the Russell 3000 Aerospace and Defense index have delivered healthy outperformance vis-à-vis their parent indices during the last one, three and five years.

Relative performance: European and US aerospace and defence indices

Source: LSEG, total returns in USD as of July 31, 2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future returns. Please see the end for important legal disclosures.

Taking a view on defense

Amidst rising security needs, growing political uncertainty and the possibility of long-term government contracts, the Aerospace and Defense sector is an area of focus for equity market participants. Companies within the sector continue to drive technological innovation and its performance attributes support portfolio diversification. FTSE Russell’s indices can help you track the performance and risk of aerospace and defence stocks with precision.

Citations

[1] Global defense spending soars to new high

[2] What Trump said about Canada, Mexico, NATO and Gaza hostages at news conference | Reuters

[3] See 240617-def-exp-2024-en.pdf
